chapter_summary: In this section, Hirsch identifies developmentalism and individualism as the unexamined ideas governing American education, arguing that child-centered pedagogy is rooted in a quasi-religious faith in natural providence. He counters this 'Pelagian' view of innate childhood goodness with evidence from evolutionary psychology and historical theology, asserting that human nature is a mixture of selfish and altruistic impulses that requires active adult guidance and schooling to shape.

claims.text: The most important ideas governing human affairs are often simple ones that remain unexamined, specifically developmentalism and individualism.
claims.source_passage: Some of the most important ideas that govern human affairs are sim�ple ones – so simple that they remain unexamined. In this chapter I’ll deal with two such ideas: developmentalism and individualism.

claims.text: Current American individualism, which prioritizes the 'I' over the 'WE', has been fostered by the individualistic doctrines of developmentalism.
claims.source_passage: My point will be that the current individualism in America which elevates the ‘I’ over the ‘WE’ has been helped along by the individualistic doctrines of de�velopmentalism

claims.text: Developmentalist doctrines completely took over American elementary education by the mid-twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: the individualistic doctrines of de�velopmentalism which completely took over our elementary education by mid-twentieth century.

claims.text: Child-centered education rests on the unstated religious assumption that nature is the agent of God's providence and therefore children's instincts are inherently right.
claims.source_passage: Behind that principle is another, unstated one: The child’s instincts and powers come from nature, and nature is the agent of God’s providence. That unstated religious faith in the rightness of nature has been a powerful force in mod�ern American life.

claims.text: Earlier American education viewed child instincts as a 'mixed affair' that required content and guidance from adult teachers.
claims.source_passage: Our earlier view (just check those earlier American schoolbooks) had been that the child’s instincts and powers are a mixed affair which need to be given content and guidance. Therefore, we concluded, it is incumbent upon adult teachers to lead the child in the right direction.

claims.text: Developmentalism assumes that because children's inborn natures are different and divine, those differences must be respected, thereby fueling individualism.
claims.source_passage: And, since individ�ual children’s inborn natures are different, those natural differences shall be respected, precisely because they are inborn and from God. Hence, we honor individualism.

claims.text: Current developmental psychology and brain science suggest that children's inborn instincts are complex and not purely benign.
claims.source_passage: But the latest work in developmental psychology and brain science paints a more complex, less benign picture of the child’s inborn instincts and powers.

claims.text: The human child is born with conflicting impulses of selfish individualism and empathetic altruism.
claims.source_passage: In effect, this current work in psychology supports the idea that the human child is born with conflicting impulses of selfish individualism and empathetic altruism.

claims.text: It is the role of schooling to influence how the child's selfish and unselfish instincts comport themselves within the individual and the culture.
claims.source_passage: Both are inborn, and it is up to human schooling to influence how they comport themselves within the child, and within the wider culture.

claims.text: Modern science, earlier American traditions, and major literary figures like Shakespeare and Goethe dissent from the current faith in natural development.
claims.source_passage: All this is relevant to our current educational policy because current science – and our earlier American traditions, which built the nation – dissent from our current faith in natural development.

claims.text: Hyper-individualism (prioritizing the 'I' over the 'WE') has recently dominated in America and weakened national cohesion.
claims.source_passage: hyper-individualism (the “I” over the “WE”) has recently dominated in America and has weakened our cohesion.

claims.text: If left to chance, selfish individualism is likely to dominate over altruism.
claims.source_passage: Left to chance, it’s probable that selfish individualism will dominate

claims.text: The choice to move away from developmentalism is a matter of pragmatism regarding school quality rather than a simple liberal vs. conservative debate.
claims.source_passage: This is not a matter of liberal vs conservative ideas – those two bins into which we currently toss incoming ideas, allowing us not to think. It’s a matter of pragmatism.

claims.text: A child's natural instincts and impulses are an unformed mixture of selfishness and altruism rather than being inherently providential.
claims.source_passage: assuming that the child’s natural instincts and impulses are providential and are to be honored and followed when in fact they are an unformed mixture of selfishness and altruism mixed uncertainly?

claims.text: Developmental education has been a primary cause of the downswing in American literacy scores.
claims.source_passage: Developmental ed­ucation has been a cause of our downswing in literacy scores!

claims.text: Literacy depends on shared background knowledge, which constitutes a shared ethnicity.
claims.source_passage: literacy depends on shared background knowledge – shared ethnicity.

claims.text: Reading scores rise dramatically when schools teach the communicative store of shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: When, by contrast, schools teach the communicative store of shared knowledge that constitutes an ethnicity, reading scores rise dramatically

claims.text: Developmentalism should be removed from the school system starting in kindergarten.
claims.source_passage: The re­moval of that incubus needs to begin in kindergarten.

claims.text: Public kindergartens are often a waste of time for children because of their pedagogical theories, not a lack of resources.
claims.source_passage: they have come to think that the kindergar­tens of the public school are a waste of valuable time – not because those schools lack resources and attractive surroundings. The public kindergar­tens have those! It’s because parents have observed the big difference in methods and results and have come to disagree with the pedagogical theory of the public school!

claims.text: Teachers are being taught that self-esteem is the most generative principle of natural human learning and development.
claims.source_passage: our teachers are being taught that the most generative principle of natural human learning and development is self-esteem.

claims.text: Gaining a particular tribal or national culture is not a natural unfolding in the same sense as physical development.
claims.source_passage: It’s quite another to describe as natural the “unfolding” of a child into gaining a particular tribal or national culture.

claims.text: Human mental development is inherently variable and artificial because it is imposed from the outside rather than developed from within.
claims.source_passage: That distinction between physical and cultural “development” makes the path of human mental development inherently variable, and artifi­cial – something imposed from the outside, not developed from within.

claims.text: American parents believe the primary aim of kindergarten is to begin learning reading, writing, and arithmetic.
claims.source_passage: American parents take the common-sense view that yes, the main aim of kindergarten is to begin learning reading, writing, and arithmetic

claims.text: Teachers are trained to believe that direct instruction in literacy and math is harmful to a child's proper psychological development.
claims.source_passage: their children’s teachers are being taught in their training that prematurely to impose such material directly, instead of allowing the child to develop those concepts naturally is harmful to the proper psychological development of the child.

claims.text: The educational devotion to the idea of natural development has gravely weakened American national literacy and national unity.
claims.source_passage: So powerful is our schools’ devotion to the idea of natural development that it has gravely weakened our national literacy, and with it, our national unity.

claims.text: There is a significant incongruence between kindergarten teachers' perspectives on educational goals and those of parents.
claims.source_passage: The results reveal an incongruence between K-teachers’ perspectives and parents.

claims.text: Kindergarten teachers prioritize self-esteem as the most important educational goal while viewing literacy and mathematics as the least important.
claims.source_passage: K-teachers view positive self-esteem as the most important goal and promoting literacy and mathematics skills as the least important.

claims.text: The 'natural-growth metaphor' remains powerfully dominant among American teachers trained in schools of education.
claims.source_passage: The natural-growth metaphor is still powerfully dominant among our teachers who have been trained in many of our schools of education.

claims.text: Literacy and the emotion of tribal unity are both dependent on shared background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The two are connected, because literacy depends on shared background knowledge, and so does the emotion of tribal unity.

claims.text: Prioritizing self-esteem as a primary educational goal is a direct consequence of a developmental conception of childhood education.
claims.source_passage: Making self-esteem the primary goal is consistent with a developmental conception of childhood education.

claims.text: The claim that mental and moral development is a universal, natural process is contradicted by history and anthropology.
claims.source_passage: But the claim of universal naturalness is problematic – as the disciplines of history and anthropology disclose.

claims.text: Human moral development is characterized more by tribal difference than by universal human sameness.
claims.source_passage: In human moral development, tribal difference, not human sameness, takes center stage.

claims.text: Basic cognitive functions, such as number sense, develop differently across different societies.
claims.source_passage: But it’s not just moral development that differs in different societies; even basic development in something as neutral as number sense develops very differently in different societies

claims.text: Child-centered and self-esteem-centered educational theories achieved total dominance in American elementary education by the mid-20th century.
claims.source_passage: Unfortunately, the child-centered, self-esteem-centered conception of development fully took over our elementary education by the mid-20th century

claims.text: The growth of concepts in a child is an arduous mental process of symbol manipulation rather than a natural, plant-like organic development.
claims.source_passage: the 'growth' of concepts in the child is not an organic development, but an arduous mental process based upon the active manipulation of symbols by the infant in alliance with sensory inputs; it is not an internal 'development' like the growth of a plant from a seed

claims.text: The physical change in classroom furniture layout (away from rows) was driven by shifts in educational philosophy.
claims.source_passage: When the education philosophy changed, (which caused our reading scores to decline) so did the furniture change.

claims.text: The change in educational philosophy—rather than the change in physical classroom environment—caused American reading scores to decline.
claims.source_passage: That physical change did not make our reading scores decline. But the reasons for the furniture change did make the scores decline.

claims.text: Romantic developmentalism is the single greatest barrier to educational equality and social justice in modern education.
claims.source_passage: Romantic developmentalism (an educational idea that flourished in Europe in the early nineteenth century and took over American elementary education) is the single greatest barrier to educational equality and social justice in modern education.

claims.text: Developmentalism infantilizes, individualizes, and delays early education rather than integrating children into the social world.
claims.source_passage: It infantilizes and individualizes and delays early education instead of quickly and equally bringing young people into the social world they are to inhabit.

claims.text: Developmentalism prevents schools from overcoming the disadvantages students face in their home environments.
claims.source_passage: It hinders the school from overcoming home disadvantage so that all students can master the public sphere.

claims.text: The rise of developmentalism as a guiding principle in early education is the greatest tragedy of American public schooling in the last century.
claims.source_passage: The gravest tragedy of American public schooling in the past 100 years has been the rise of this “developmentalism” as a chief guiding principle of early education.

claims.text: Developmentalism sustains the verbal gap between Black and White students.
claims.source_passage: It sustains the black/white verbal gap.

claims.text: Developmentalism has led to a miseducated adult population, evidenced by public misunderstanding of the COVID pandemic.
claims.source_passage: It has miseducated our adult population – as recent interviews about the COVID pandemic have illustrated.

claims.text: The 1983 report 'A Nation at Risk' failed to produce permanent reform because its analysis was high-school-centric and ignored early childhood education.
claims.source_passage: The reason that A Nation at Risk did not produce a more permanent reform... is this: The study group for A Nation at Risk was controlled by estimable people who were presidents of top universities, chairmen of corporations, and high school principals. The college presidents set the tone. The analysis was high-school-centric.

claims.text: Adult achievement is heavily determined by the level of background knowledge acquired during early childhood.
claims.source_passage: If only those 1983 university presidents knew then what we know now about the degree to which adult achievement is determined by early background knowledge, their report might have induced a reform of our elementary schools.

claims.text: Elementary teacher training is disproportionately focused on educational methods at the expense of subject matter knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The teacher preparation curriculum is weighted heavily with “educational methods” at the expense of courses in subjects to be taught.

claims.text: Approximately 41 percent of the time for elementary school teacher candidates is spent in education courses.
claims.source_passage: A survey of 1,350 institutions training teachers indicated that 41 percent of the time of elementary school teacher candidates is spent in education courses.

claims.text: The primary cause of American grade-school failure since the 1950s is the adoption of developmentalism in early grades.
claims.source_passage: The chief agency of our grade-school failures since the 1950s has been the developmentalism of the early grades.

claims.text: Developmentalism gained full control over American schools and teacher training institutions during the second half of the twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: Developmentalism fully took over our schools and our teacher training institutions in the second half of the twentieth century, when (as the previous SAT chart indicates) the decline began in a big way.

claims.text: The physical layout of child-centered classrooms, using tables instead of desks, makes teacher-led instruction physically awkward.
claims.source_passage: Even if a school decided to introduce a definite curriculum that is teacher-led rather than child-centered, we would still need to use this furniture. Currently, that’s a little awkward.

claims.text: The decline in American SAT scores began at the same time developmentalism became the dominant educational philosophy.
claims.source_passage: the second half of the twentieth century, when (as the previous SAT chart indicates) the decline began in a big way.

claims.text: Elizabeth Peabody was potentially more influential than John Dewey or William Heard Kilpatrick in bringing developmental ideas into American schools.
claims.source_passage: there was also another early player who may have been even more influential – Elizabeth Peabody, who introduced the developmental ideas of Friedrich Froebel.

claims.text: The Common School philosophy was the primary educational model that created the United States.
claims.source_passage: But first let’s consider the school philosophy that came before developmentalism–and created the United States.

claims.text: Early Massachusetts schools were integrated and required all students, including free black students, to attend the Common School.
claims.source_passage: Earlier in Massachusetts... there was not even school segregation of black students. All young people, including free black students, were required to attend the Common School.

claims.text: Horace Mann consciously rejected the developmentalist ideas of Jean Jacques Rousseau.
claims.source_passage: Mann knew of the originator of developmentalist ideas, Jean Jacques Rousseau, whose Émile had been published in 1763, but he rejected those ideas.

claims.text: Human beings are less naturally endowed with guidance-providing instincts than lower animals and are born into a 'universe of darkness'.
claims.source_passage: The human being is less endowed with instincts for his guidance than the lower orders of animated creation... it is to him a universe of darkness.

claims.text: All human faculties and powers are susceptible to either right or wrong direction and control.
claims.source_passage: All his faculties and powers are susceptible of a right direction and control... But all his powers and faculties are also liable to a wrong direction and control

claims.text: James Madison's view of human nature as 'double-sided' is consistent with modern evolutionary psychology.
claims.source_passage: Madison’s balanced view is essentially that of present-day evolutionary psychologists, who have explained why humans have evolved the double sidedness that Madison described.

claims.text: Human nature contains both innate selfish tendencies and innate altruistic tendencies because both had survival value.
claims.source_passage: Madison and Mann were right because both the selfish and altruistic sides of human nature have evolved successfully because both sides have had survival value.

claims.text: The American founders emphasized schooling as a means to inculcate altruism and loyalty to the nation over natural selfishness.
claims.source_passage: they placed a great deal of emphasis on schooling to inculcate altruism and loyalty to the whole tribe... to develop the altruistic and patriotic side of human nature rather than the selfish side.

claims.text: Students in a republic should be taught that they are public property and belong to the state rather than themselves.
claims.source_passage: Let our pupil be taught that he does not belong to himself, but that he is public property.

claims.text: The town of Concord, Massachusetts, specifically the followers of Emerson and Thoreau, became the center for introducing nature worship and developmentalism into American schools.
claims.source_passage: The successors to Horace Mann in Massachusetts – especially in the town of Concord, – the home of Emerson and Thoreau – made the state also a cradle of the new nature worship in America.

claims.text: Friedrich Froebel's metaphor of the 'kindergarten' implies that children should be gardened as natural beings rather than socialized through instruction.
claims.source_passage: kindergarten... That’s a German word that literally means 'children’s garden.' That is to say: a place where you garden children. Froebel chose that metaphor as ideal

claims.text: Horace Mann, Benjamin Rush, and other early American leaders did not adhere to the 'religion of nature' inspired by Rousseau.
claims.source_passage: ann and Rush and other American leaders were not adherents to the religion of nature that swept Europe after Rousseau in the nineteenth century.

claims.text: Elizabeth Peabody was a primary figure in introducing developmentalist principles and the ideas of Friedrich Froebel to American education.
claims.source_passage: She was a prime introducer of the developmental idea to our teachers and parents. She was the introducer of Friedrich Froebel into our intellectual bloodstream.

claims.text: The term 'kindergarten' (children's garden) was chosen by Froebel as a metaphor for the principle of 'gardening' children rather than instructing them.
claims.source_passage: Froebel was the German writer who invented and named the kindergarten. That’s a German word that literally means “children’s garden.” That is to say: a place where you garden children. Froebel chose that metaphor as ideal for the developmentalist principle.

claims.text: Modern educational terms like 'constructivism' are neutral abstractions that mask an underlying Froebelian religious confidence in natural development.
claims.source_passage: They use neutral abstractions like “constructivism” and the like. But behind the conviction that “self-esteem” trumps “rote learning” lies a firm, Froebelist faith in natural development.

claims.text: The core religious faith of developmentalism is an intense belief in the unspoiled and holy character of the child's inborn instinct.
claims.source_passage: This faith in the child’s inborn instinct is the intense faith in the unspoiled and holy character of the natural. That is the central religious faith of developmentalism.

claims.text: The developmentalist faith held by educators in 2022 is as intense as the faith held in 1890.
claims.source_passage: It is a faith that is held as intensely in 2022 as it was held in 1890.

claims.text: John Dewey's 1897 'Pedagogic Creed' was even more influential than Elizabeth Peabody's work in establishing developmentalism.
claims.source_passage: In 1897 John Dewey followed up Ms. Peabody’s profession of faith with his own, even more influential developmental Pedagogic Creed.

claims.text: According to Dewey, education must connect with a child's own initiative; otherwise, it is merely 'pressure from without' and is not truly educative.
claims.source_passage: Save as the efforts of the educator connect with some activity which the child is carrying on of his own initiative independent of the educator, education becomes reduced to a pressure from without. It may, indeed, give certain external results but cannot truly be called educative.

claims.text: John Dewey believed that social mastery is instinctive and does not require assertive instruction from a teacher.
claims.source_passage: He does not think any special indoctrination is needed. Mastery of the social will be instinctive. No need for assertive instruction by the teacher.

claims.text: Dewey's theory characterized social indoctrination as a natural, developmental process rather than an external imposition.
claims.source_passage: Dewey has made even social indoctrination developmental. It develops on its own, as part of a natural process.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick's 1918 article 'The Project Method' was a nation-changing document that institutionalized a naturalistic approach to knowledge-gain.
claims.source_passage: William Heard Kilpatrick in the year 1918, writing from Teachers College, Columbia University, issued a nation-changing article, “The Project Method.” It recommended a naturalistic, developmental approach to early knowledge-gain

claims.text: John Dewey conceptualized social indoctrination as a developmental, natural process that unfolds within the child.
claims.source_passage: Dewey has made even social indoctrination developmental. It develops on its own, as part of a natural process.

claims.text: Dewey's developmentalism fails to acknowledge that a child's instincts might be inherently selfish or evil and in need of correction.
claims.source_passage: That’s a bit more complex than Froebel but by no means acknowledges that the child’s instincts and powers might be inherently selfish and evil, and in need of correction.

claims.text: The terms 'Constructivism,' 'Problem-Based Learning,' and 'Discovery Learning' are contemporary aliases for the original 'Project Method.'
claims.source_passage: The project method is now called ‘Constructivism,’ ‘Problem-Based Learning,’ ‘Discovery Learning,’ as well as other names, too.

claims.text: Educational theorists rename the Project Method whenever its failures are accurately reported.
claims.source_passage: Many other names, for as soon as somebody accurately reports its failures, it gets an additional name.

claims.text: Controlled studies by mainline researchers invariably show negative results for the effectiveness of minimal guidance in instruction.
claims.source_passage: When mainline researchers study its effectiveness in properly controlled studies, the results are invariably negative.

claims.text: Studies reported by adherents of developmental methods do not include credible success stories among disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: But even the favorable studies reported by adherents do not tend to include credible success stories among disadvantaged students.

claims.text: The triumph of developmental methods in elementary schools caused a decline in all American reading scores, including those of white students, in the twentieth century.
claims.source_passage: With the gradual triumph of ‘developmental’ methods in elementary school, all American reading scores – including those of white students – declined in the twentieth century.

claims.text: Current American reading scores remain low because of the continued dominance of developmentalism.
claims.source_passage: Today’s reading scores remain low because of the dominance of developmentalism.

claims.text: Developmentalism is maintained by teacher instructors with religious conviction because its faith in nature is theological in origin.
claims.source_passage: It is held by them with religious conviction because its faith in nature is religious. Its theology assumes that its naturalistic approach is right because nature is God’s agent on earth, and natural development follows God’s plan.

claims.text: The human neocortex is neither instinctual nor pre-programmed; it is essentially a 'blank page' waiting to acquire and integrate material.
claims.source_passage: That neocortex, where schooling mainly takes place, is neither instinctual nor pre-programed... The latest evolved part of the brain – the neocortex–is basically a blank page ready to be written upon.

claims.text: Evolutionary adaptation favors the ability to accommodate to current circumstances over being born with a rigid developmental blueprint.
claims.source_passage: Nature, through evolution, has determined that inventing how to accommodate to current circumstances is more helpful to survival than being born with a developmental blueprint.

claims.text: A young child’s actual instinct is to wait for instructions which they then eagerly assimilate and correlate.
claims.source_passage: The young child’s actual instinct is to wait for instructions – which it then eagerly assimilates and actively correlates.

claims.text: American education schools require human development textbooks because 'development' is their foundational thought model, regardless of its scientific validity.
claims.source_passage: But why? Even though such books are enormously expensive and time-consuming, they are assigned because ‘development’ is the thought model of the American education school.

claims.text: Current teacher textbooks attempt to reconcile outdated Piagetian theory with modern science by 'having it both ways' to satisfy both scientists and education professors.
claims.source_passage: The authors have it both ways in this 2019 edition, one way for the knowledgeable scientist who has repudiated the stage theory of Piaget, and another to please the education professor who is devoted to developmentalism.

claims.text: A child's effort to understand her world is far more social and influenced by cultural values than Piaget's theory described.
claims.source_passage: In reality, a child’s effort to understand her world is far more social than Piaget described. Her growing understanding of the world is profoundly influenced by interactions with family members, peers, and teachers, and it takes place against the backdrop of cultural values.

claims.text: Piaget's theory remains the most complete account of cognitive development despite its relative neglect of social and cultural forces.
claims.source_passage: As noted previously, it remains the most complete account of cognitive development.

claims.text: Current teacher textbooks contradict themselves by acknowledging that culture overrides nature while simultaneously maintaining that developmental 'unfolding' is universal.
claims.source_passage: You can’t logically say that “unfolding” is universal and then say that the “unfolding” is not universal but is dependent on culture.

claims.text: Human development is not a process of internal 'unfolding' but is fundamentally language-bound and knowledge-bound.
claims.source_passage: Human development is language bound and knowledge bound.

claims.text: Schools are not facilitating universal unfolding but are instructing children in a specific national ethnicity.
claims.source_passage: The schools of different nations are not assisting in a universal unfolding. Rather, they are instructing children in an ethnicity – how to become mature, contributing members of a particular nation

claims.text: The triumph of developmentalism in the United States has caused reading ability and language skills to decline in recent decades.
claims.source_passage: Because of the triumph of developmentalism in the United States, reading ability and language skill in the United States in recent decades have gone downhill

claims.text: The persistence of the reading gap between poor and advantaged children is due to the educational system's faith in natural development over instruction.
claims.source_passage: Our reading decline and our persistent unfairness between groups are both owing to our current faith in the superiority of natural development over artificial instruction.

claims.text: Literacy is an evolutionary recent development for which no internal biological blueprint exists.
claims.source_passage: But there is no internal blueprint for literacy – which arrived on the evolutionary scene far too recently for current nature to take notice.

claims.text: Direct, active instruction from the outside is the only effective way to achieve literacy.
claims.source_passage: Active instruction of the child from the outside is the only literacy blueprint available.

claims.text: Literacy instruction must explicitly include a tribe's traditions and moral imperatives because these do not develop naturally.
claims.source_passage: Literacy instruction must include not only the language of the tribe, but also the traditions, and moral imperatives of the tribe. These do not unfold. They must be explicitly taught.

claims.text: Education professors subscribe to a false faith that nature is benign and therefore natural education is moral education.
claims.source_passage: They subscribe to the false faith that because nature is God’s providential agent, nature is therefore benign. Hence natural education is moral education, according to Dewey and his successors.

claims.text: Developmentalism is a modern version of the Pelagian heresy, which assumes human nature is naturally good and reliable.
claims.source_passage: theologically speaking, developmentalism is an updated version of the Pelagian heresy that human nature if allowed to develop naturally is good and can be relied on.

claims.text: Nature is not a reliable guide to anything much in human schooling, including readiness, reading, and math.
claims.source_passage: Nature is not a reliable guide to anything much in human schooling – not to “readiness,” not to reading, not to math.

claims.text: Math instruction depends on a child's ethnicity/culture rather than a universal natural unfolding.
claims.source_passage: Even math instruction turns out to depend on the child’s ethnicity, as Professor Stevenson and his colleagues have shown.

claims.text: The nineteenth-century 'religion of nature' is inconsistent with findings from current evolutionary psychology, sociobiology, and brain studies.
claims.source_passage: Its slowness and confident laissez faire is inconsistent not just with the ancient philosophers – but as we now understand, with nature herself, according to current evolutionary psychology, sociobiology, and current brain studies.

claims.text: Human success is a result of artificial, non-instinctual activities rather than accord with nature.
claims.source_passage: Our artificiality, not our accord with nature, has done the trick.

claims.text: Naturalistic, project-based learning is elitist and does not work with equal effect upon rich and poor students.
claims.source_passage: Naturalistic, project-based learning is, moreover, elitist. It does not work efficiently, nor with equal effect upon rich and poor.

claims.text: Project-based, constructivist learning is the enemy of fairness.
claims.source_passage: Project-based, constructivist learning is the enemy of fairness.

claims.text: Schools continue to adhere to debunked naturalistic theories because education schools teach that the natural is inherently good and the artificial is bad.
claims.source_passage: I would say: “It’s mainly because our education schools teach, and our schools continue to believe, that the natural is good and the artificial is bad.”

claims.text: Nature has withheld reliable instinctive guidance from the human child regarding social and academic development.
claims.source_passage: But as Horace Mann correctly observed, nature has withheld reliable instinctive guidance from the human child.

claims.text: The developmentalist excuse of 'home disadvantage' is logically inconsistent with the theory that learning develops naturally from internal instincts.
claims.source_passage: Project-based, discovery learning holds that the child’s own “instincts and powers” will make learning develop naturally. Yet, when it comes to excusing the schools’ dismal results for disadvantaged children, the adherents of developmentalism concede that those children were prevented from unfolding naturally, because they lacked the rich surroundings of the advantaged home.

claims.text: Effective, non-developmental schooling starting in kindergarten can overcome home disadvantages in language and knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, good, non-developmental, non-project-based schooling, starting in kindergarten, does gradually overcome home disadvantage in effective schools in the early grades.

claims.text: By grade six, disadvantaged children in effective schools can outperform advantaged children who received a child-centered developmental education.
claims.source_passage: In fact, by grade six, disadvantaged children are ahead of their advantaged age group who have undergone a child-centered developmental education down the street.

claims.text: Early human education does not consist of an unfolding in chronological stages.
claims.source_passage: For, it is demonstrable that early human education does not consist of an unfolding in stages

claims.text: Children’s progress in school is more dependent on prior knowledge than on their age.
claims.source_passage: and that children’s progress in the school is less dependent on the child’s age than on what the child has already learned.

claims.text: Discovery centers are suboptimal pedagogical tools compared to whole-class learning.
claims.source_passage: Discovery centers are suboptimal compared to whole-class learning.

claims.text: Language proficiency, rather than chronological age, is the determining factor in educational attainment.
claims.source_passage: Professor Susan Carey, whose brilliant research has established that language proficiency, not age, determines educational attainment.

claims.text: Focusing early education on the child's construction of knowledge through their own efforts is a 'profligate waste of time'.
claims.source_passage: To focus early education on “the construction of knowledge” by the child’s own efforts is a profligate waste of time that penalizes any child, rich or poor.

claims.text: Susan Neuman advocates for explicit contextualizing of word meanings alongside implicit modes of developmental pedagogy like discovery learning.
claims.source_passage: For, along with her advocacy of explicit contextualizing of word meanings, she also advocates implicit modes of developmental pedagogy like discovery learning

claims.text: Discovery centers are a defining characteristic of constructivist developmentalism.
claims.source_passage: Discovery centers are a hallmark of constructivist developmentalism.

claims.text: Discovery centers are less effective for learning than whole-class instruction.
claims.source_passage: Discovery centers are suboptimal compared to whole-class learning.

claims.text: Language is the fundamental medium of human society and human learning.
claims.source_passage: Language is the lifeblood of human society and of human learning

claims.text: Discovery-based learning methods punish poor children more severely than wealthy children.
claims.source_passage: But it punishes the poor much more than the rich because poor children are getting ineffective instruction in both the home and the school, while rich children are gaining lots of knowledge at home.

claims.text: Poor children receive ineffective instruction in both their home and school environments.
claims.source_passage: poor children are getting ineffective instruction in both the home and the school

claims.text: Wealthy children acquire significant amounts of knowledge through their home environment.
claims.source_passage: rich children are gaining lots of knowledge at home.

claims.text: Developmentalism is characterized by slowness and inefficiency that harms the nations that adopt it.
claims.source_passage: The slowness and inefficiency of developmentalism penalizes any nation that adopts it.

claims.text: The adoption of developmentalism is the primary reason for the United States' low standing in international PISA rankings.
claims.source_passage: It’s the chief case of our poor showing (twenty-fifth among the nations on the international PISA rankings).

claims.text: The black/white language gap is primarily caused by developmentalist educational practices.
claims.source_passage: It’s the chief cause of the black/white language gap.

claims.text: The language gap between racial groups is a problem that can be resolved.
claims.source_passage: It is a gap we shall overcome.

claims.text: The cultural preference for organic foods is an unexamined nod to the providential character of nature.
claims.source_passage: We nod to nature’s providential character every time we choose “organic” foods, and every time we choose a natural approach to early childhood education.

claims.text: Standard teacher training materials identify four primary forces generating human development: biological, psychological, sociocultural, and life-cycle forces.
claims.source_passage: According to that view, human development is being generated by four forces: biological, psychological, sociocultural, and life-cycle forces.

claims.text: It is logically contradictory to classify the 'socio-cultural' as a natural force of developmental 'unfolding' equivalent to biological maturation.
claims.source_passage: But note how unnatural is one of the listed forces – the “socio-cultural.” It’s one thing to describe the universal unfolding of physical development into puberty. It’s quite another to describe as natural the “unfolding” of a child into gaining a particular tribal or national culture.

claims.text: Child self-esteem typically remains high in the preschool years but stabilizes at the beginning of adolescence.
claims.source_passage: Self-esteem is normally very high in preschool children but declines gradually during the early elementary school years as children compare themselves to others. By the beginning of adolescence, self-esteem has usually stabilized—it neither increases nor decreases in these years (Harter, Whitesell, & Kowalski, 1992).

claims.text: Kohlberg’s eight stages of moral unfolding represents the dominant moral framework currently taught in teacher training programs.
claims.source_passage: The chief moral idea is that of Kohlberg’s universal, eight stages of moral unfolding.

claims.text: The decline in SAT verbal scores experienced a temporary leveling out following the publication of 'A Nation at Risk' in 1983.
claims.source_passage: After the report, we were not able to change the downward fall of those dismal results. But the descent of SAT verbal scores did level out for a while before the descent started again.

claims.text: Frederick Douglass was drawn to make his home in Massachusetts specifically because of the state's lack of school segregation.
claims.source_passage: All young people, including free black students, were required to attend the Common School. (The acceptance of black equality in Massachusetts was one reason the escaped slave Frederick Douglass first made his home there.)

claims.text: Citizens in a republic must be taught to prioritize the welfare of the country over familial loyalty when the state's liberty requires it.
claims.source_passage: Let him be taught to love his family but let him be taught at the same time that he must forsake and even forget them when the welfare of his country requires it.

claims.text: Naturalistic education models assume that a child, like a product of nature, will automatically will what is best for themselves if left undisturbed.
claims.source_passage: This necessity must presuppose that the still young human being, even though as yet unconsciously, like a product of Nature, precisely and surely wills that which is best for himself...

claims.text: Evolutionary history suggests that the capacity to invent accommodations to new circumstances is more valuable for survival than rigid, inborn developmental blueprints.
claims.source_passage: Nature, through evolution, has determined that inventing how to accommodate to current circumstances is more helpful to survival than being born with a developmental blueprint.

claims.text: Chinese children learn to count to 20 months earlier than English learners, whereas American children learn the meanings of 'one' and 'two' six months earlier than Chinese children.
claims.source_passage: advantaged Chinese children learn to count to 20 many months earlier than do advantaged English learners... However, American children learn the meaning of “one” and “two” six months earlier than Chinese children do.

claims.text: Effective early moral education requires the use of 'customs' and 'moral restraints' to constrain impulsive and selfish natural behavior.
claims.source_passage: “The curiosity of nations” – that means a society’s customs, its laws and moral restraints that hold a society together. These curiosities constrain impulse and cheating...

claims.text: Spelling and phonics must be taught directly rather than being left to unfold naturally through developmental stages.
claims.source_passage: Professor John McWhorter of Columbia University... recently felt he needed to write a piece explaining yet again why spelling and phonics need to be taught directly and not encouraged to unfold “naturally” and “developmentally.”

claims.text: Developmentalists use the concept of 'home disadvantage' as an inconsistent excuse to explain why children do not unfold at a uniform natural pace.
claims.source_passage: Developmentalists excuse its unfair results by blaming home “disadvantage,” which is described as an insurmountable barrier. Hence, they say it’s ok to persist in the grave slowness and ineffectiveness of developmental education. But note the inconsistency in that view!

evidence.description: The theoretical foundation of sociobiology stating that selfishness beats altruism within groups, but altruistic groups beat selfish groups.
evidence.source_reference: David Sloan Wilson and E. O. Wilson: ‘Rethinking the Theoretical Foundations of Sociobiology,’ 2007
evidence.source_passage: ‘Selfishness beats altruism within groups. Altruistic groups beat selfish groups. Every�thing else is commentary.’

evidence.description: Historical debate between St. Augustine and Pelagius over the innate innocence versus 'infected mixture' (original sin) of human nature.
evidence.source_reference: St. Augustine, On the Proceedings Concerning Pelagius
evidence.source_passage: Augustine (a good observer of the dualism in humans) said: ‘Yes! We are still guilty of Adam’s disobedience and are still an infected mixture of good and bad.’

evidence.description: Literary evidence of 'human doubleness' from Shakespeare (Sonnet 144) and Goethe (Faust).
evidence.source_reference: Shakespeare, Sonnet 144; Goethe, Faust
evidence.source_passage: Shakespeare was also an anti-Pe�lagian who emphasized our innate mixture of good and evil... And there’s a memorable moment in Goethe’s Faust, when Faust complains that ‘two souls dwell in my breast.’

evidence.description: Statistical and sociological data showing an 'upswing' in hyper-individualism and a decline in national cohesion.
evidence.source_reference: Robert D. Putnam, The Upswing, 2020
evidence.source_passage: Robert Putnam observes in his new book Upswing, which shows that hyper-individualism (the ‘I’ over the ‘WE’) has recently dominated in America and has weak�ened our cohesion.

evidence.description: Robert Putnam's data showing a historical shift toward hyper-individualism in America.
evidence.source_reference: Robert D. Putnam, Upswing (2020)
evidence.source_passage: Robert Putnam ob­serves in his new book Upswing, which shows that hyper-individualism (the “I” over the “WE”) has recently dominated in America and has weak­ened our cohesion.

evidence.description: 25,000 parents in the South Bronx applying for only 135 spots in Core Knowledge schools.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (referencing previous chapter)
evidence.source_passage: parents of some 25,000 children in the South Bronx try every year to place their child in one of the seven Bronx Core Knowledge (CK) schools where there are just 135 kindergar­ten places.

evidence.description: A study showing teachers prioritize self-esteem while parents prioritize literacy and math in kindergarten.
evidence.source_reference: Sverdlov, A., & Aram, D. (2016)
evidence.source_passage: K-teachers view positive self-esteem as the most important goal and promoting literacy and mathematics skills as the least import­ant. Parents believe, however, advancement in literacy and mathe­mati­cs skills are the most important goal.

evidence.description: A widely used teacher textbook revised in 2019 that emphasizes natural development and self-esteem.
evidence.source_reference: Kail, Robert V.; Cavanaugh, John C. Human Development: A Life-Span View (2019)
evidence.source_passage: I base my claim that developmental determinism and the primacy of self- esteem are still being taught by analyzing a still-widely used teacher textbook revised in 2019: Kail, Robert V.; Cavanaugh, John C.

evidence.description: Scientific work suggesting the neocortical microcircuit is a tabula rasa rather than pre-programmed for specific culture.
evidence.source_reference: Kalisman, Silberberg, Markram, and Merzenich (2005)
evidence.source_passage: This is exactly the conclusion being reached by the latest thinking in develop­mental psychology – especially in the work of the three scientists and their colleagues often cited in this book: Susan Carey, Nir Kalisman, and Harold Stevenson.

evidence.description: An analysis of a representative 2019 teacher training textbook which contains 97 references to self-esteem but none to patriotism.
evidence.source_reference: Kail, Robert V.; Cavanaugh, John C. Human Development: A Life-Span View (2019)

evidence.description: Cross-cultural research showing that basic number sense development varies significantly across different societies.
evidence.source_reference: Harold Stevenson and his associates (1990)

evidence.description: Historical analysis of the rise of individualism in American life and education during the mid-20th century.
evidence.source_reference: Robert Putnam, The Upswing (2020)

evidence.description: Cognitive science research demonstrating that conceptual 'growth' is a constructive effort involving symbol manipulation.
evidence.source_reference: Susan Carey, The Origin of Concepts (2009)

evidence.description: Chart showing the decline of SAT Verbal scores by decade from 1952 to 2012.
The 1983 U.S. Government report 'A Nation at Risk' which highlighted declining educational performance.
evidence.source_reference: A Nation at Risk (1983)

evidence.description: A survey of 1,350 teacher training institutions showing that 41 percent of candidate time is spent on education courses.
A 1940 'March of Time' program featuring William Heard Kilpatrick and John Dewey demonstrating enthusiasm for developmentalism.
evidence.source_reference: March of Time (1940)

evidence.description: March of Time program circa 1940 showcasing the enthusiasm for developmentalism in grade schools.
evidence.source_reference: March of Time, 1940
evidence.source_passage: We can get a feel for the enthusiasm with which our grade schools adopted developmentalism by watching a March of Time program circa 1940 which featured William Heard Kilpatrick and John Dewey.

evidence.description: The historical lack of segregation for black students in Massachusetts Common Schools during the early 19th century.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch, American Ethnicity
evidence.source_passage: Earlier in Massachusetts, where leadership of the American school system resided, there was not even school segregation of black students.

evidence.description: The writings of Horace Mann in the Common School Journal regarding the necessity of guidance for human beings.
evidence.source_reference: Common School Journal (1838)
evidence.source_passage: The human being is less endowed with instincts for his guidance than the lower orders of animated creation.

evidence.description: James Madison's discussion of human nature in Federalist 55.
evidence.source_reference: Madison, Federalist 55
evidence.source_passage: As there is a degree of depravity in mankind which requires a certain degree of circumspection and distrust, so there are other qualities in human nature which justify a certain portion of esteem and confidence.

evidence.description: Sociobiological principle that altruistic groups outperform selfish groups while individuals within groups are naturally selfish.
evidence.source_reference: Wilson, David Sloan and E. O. Wilson (2007)
evidence.source_passage: Selfishness beats altruism within groups. Altruistic groups beat selfish groups. Everything else is commentary.

evidence.description: Benjamin Rush's 1789 proposal for education in a republic emphasizing state allegiance.
evidence.source_reference: Benjamin Rush (1789)
evidence.source_passage: Let our pupil be taught that he does not belong to himself, but that he is public property.

evidence.description: The translation of Friedrich Froebel by Elizabeth Peabody (1890) which argues education must be 'passive' and follow the 'divine' nature of the child.
John Dewey's 'Pedagogic Creed' (1897), which posits the child's instincts as the starting point for all education.
William Heard Kilpatrick's article 'The Project Method' (1918), cited as a pivotal influence on American pedagogy.
Analysis of a 2019 teacher textbook (Kail & Cavanaugh) used to demonstrate that developmental determinism and self-esteem primacy are still taught.
evidence.source_reference: Kail, Robert V.; Cavanaugh, John C. Human Development: A Life-Span View (2019)

evidence.description: A meta-analysis by Kirschner, Sweller, and Clark showing that minimal guidance in instruction is ineffective compared to direct instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Kirschner, Paul A., Sweller, John, and Clark, Richard E. (2006)
evidence.source_passage: A decisive summary was recently provided in a study by an international team of psychologists in an article entitled: ‘Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work’

evidence.description: The landmark 1983 report documenting the decline of American educational standards and reading scores.
evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education, A Nation at Risk (1983)
evidence.source_passage: That disaster was reported in A Nation at Risk.

evidence.description: Scientific article arguing that the microcircuitry of the neocortex functions as a tabula rasa rather than a pre-programmed system.
evidence.source_reference: Kalisman, Nir, et al., 'The Neocortical Microcircuit as a Tabula Rasa' (2005)
evidence.source_passage: The latest evolved part of the brain – the neocortex–is basically a blank page ready to be written upon, as the cognitive scientist, Nir Kalisman stated in a memorable article entitled ‘The Neocortical Microcircuit as a Tabula Rasa.’

evidence.description: The 8th edition of a standard teacher education textbook used to show how developmentalism is taught despite criticisms of Piaget.
evidence.source_reference: Kail, Robert V. and Cavanaugh, John C., Human Development: A Life-Span View (2019)
evidence.source_passage: Here’s a respectable example of 2019 from Kail and Kavenaugh... who offer a teacher’s textbook on human development that has gone through eight editions.

evidence.description: Cross-cultural study showing Chinese children count to 20 months earlier than English learners due to language characteristics, while American children learn 'one' and 'two' earlier for similar reasons.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson, Harold W., et al. (1990); LeCorre, M. et al. (2016)
evidence.source_passage: According to pathbreaking work by Harold Stevenson and his colleagues, advantaged Chinese children learn to count to 20 many months earlier than do advantaged English learners... American children learn the meaning of “one” and “two” six months earlier than Chinese children do.

evidence.description: International assessment data showing ongoing decline in US reading performance.
evidence.source_reference: PISA reports
evidence.source_passage: and as the international PISA reports continue to show.

evidence.description: Shakespearean character Edmund's 'prayer to nature' serves as a counter-example to the idea that nature is inherently benign or a good guide for morality.
evidence.source_reference: Shakespeare, King Lear
evidence.source_passage: Thou, Nature, art my goddess; to thy law My services are bound... Thus did Shakespeare accuse nature.

evidence.description: Analysis of a 2019 teacher textbook showing the persistence of Piagetian stage theory despite scientific repudiation.
evidence.source_reference: Kail, Robert V.; Cavanaugh, John C. Human Development: A Life-Span View (2019)
evidence.source_passage: The authors have it both ways in this 2019 edition, one way for the knowledgeable scientist who has repudiated the stage theory of Piaget, and another to please the education professor

evidence.description: Professor Stevenson's study showing math achievement depends on cultural/ethnic contexts rather than natural development.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson, Harold W., et al. “Contexts of Achievement” (1990)

evidence.description: Decline in international educational rankings as documentation of developmentalism's failure.
evidence.source_reference: General reference to international rankings

evidence.description: Comparison of 6th grade outcomes between disadvantaged students in effective schools and advantaged students in developmental schools.
evidence.source_reference: Chapter 1 (referenced as citation 52)

evidence.description: Research by Susan Carey establishing language proficiency as the primary driver of attainment.
evidence.source_reference: Carey, Susan, The Origin of Concepts (2009)

evidence.description: Susan Neuman's work on vocabulary, which while partially using developmental modes, demonstrates the value of explicit instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Neuman, Susan B.; Wright, Tanya S. All About Words

evidence.description: Susan Neuman's book 'All About Words' is used to demonstrate the persistence of discovery learning even among advocates of explicit instruction.
evidence.source_reference: Neuman, Susan B.; Wright, Tanya S. All About Words, 2013
evidence.source_passage: she also advocates implicit modes of developmental pedagogy like discovery learning: For instance, in All About Words, she says: 'We created a “Discovery Center” for exploring interesting new discoveries about plants.'

evidence.description: Susan Carey's research established that language proficiency, rather than biological age, determines educational success.
evidence.source_reference: Carey, S. The Origin of Concepts. New York: Oxford University Press 2009
evidence.source_passage: whose brilliant research has established that language proficiency, not age, determines educational attainment.

evidence.description: The United States ranks 25th in the international PISA rankings.
evidence.source_reference: PISA Rankings (Implicit reference to OECD data)
evidence.source_passage: our poor showing (twenty-fifth among the nations on the international PISA rankings).

concepts.author_definition: The educational doctrine that schooling should follow the child's natural instincts and powers, assuming nature is an agent of providence.
concepts.common_alternative: General term for studies in child development.
concepts.source_passage: The newer, de�velopmental view... assumes that the child’s natural instincts are providential. When they are followed with empathy, the child will flourish.

concepts.author_definition: An individualistic approach to education guided by the principle that the child's instincts and powers should dictate their learning path.
concepts.common_alternative: Modern progressive pedagogy focusing on student needs.
concepts.source_passage: Our so-called child-centered education has been individualistic education – on the principle, stated by John Dewey, that the child’s ‘instincts and powers’ should guide her education.

concepts.author_definition: The innate mixture within humans of both selfish individualism and empathetic altruism/group-centeredness.
concepts.source_passage: this current work in psychology supports the idea that the human child is born with conflicting impulses of selfish individualism and empathetic altruism.

concepts.author_definition: The view (shared by Augustine and the author) that humans are not born purely innocent but are an 'infected mixture' of good and bad requiring external guidance.
concepts.source_passage: Our deepest and most accurate writers have been anti-Pelagians like Augustine and Shakespeare, and Goethe.

concepts.author_definition: The dominant educational belief that a child's mental and cultural development unfolds naturally like a physical organism, requiring minimal direct instruction.
concepts.common_alternative: The assumption that children are naturally predisposed to learn academic skills like reading without systematic teaching.
concepts.source_passage: The natu­ral-growth metaphor is still powerfully dominant among our teachers who have been trained in many of our schools of education.

concepts.author_definition: The shared background knowledge (shared ethnicity) required for effective group communication and literacy.
A social state where the 'I' is prioritized over the 'WE,' leading to weakened social cohesion and shared identity.
concepts.source_passage: hyper-individualism (the “I” over the “WE”) has recently dominated in America and has weak­ened our cohesion.

concepts.author_definition: An educational focus where the child's internal sense of self-worth and individual trajectory are prioritized over communal knowledge or shared social goals.
The early 19th-century American school model designed to create a unified citizenry through shared moral and social instruction.
concepts.source_passage: The developmental movement took hold in the 20th century in an already successful nation... there was not even school segregation of black students... All young people... were required to attend the Common School.

concepts.author_definition: The coexistence of innate selfish and altruistic tendencies in humans, both evolved due to survival value.
concepts.common_alternative: Human nature as blank slate or purely good.
concepts.source_passage: Madison’s balanced view is essentially that of present-day evolutionary psychologists, who have explained why humans have evolved the double sidedness that Madison described.

concepts.author_definition: A 'children's garden' where children are 'gardened'—allowed to develop naturally like plants—rather than taught social knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: That’s a German word that literally means 'children’s garden.' That is to say: a place where you garden children.

concepts.author_definition: The inherent, unviolated, and healthy state of the child which is necessarily good and must not be disturbed by 'dictatorial' instruction.
concepts.source_passage: for the effect of the divine is, when undisturbed, necessarily good: in fact, it cannot be otherwise than good.

concepts.author_definition: A 'neutral abstraction' used by modern educators to describe the same underlying faith in natural development previously expressed in religious terms.
concepts.source_passage: They use neutral abstractions like “constructivism” and the like. But behind the conviction that “self-esteem” trumps “rote learning” lies a firm, Froebelist faith in natural development.

concepts.author_definition: A naturalistic, developmental approach to learning through active projects rather than direct instruction; rebranded frequently as 'Constructivism' or 'Discovery Learning.'
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes viewed as a progressive, child-centered improvement over rote memorization.
concepts.source_passage: It recommended a naturalistic, developmental approach to early knowledge-gain through working on actual projects. Kilpatrick labeled it the ‘Project Method.’

concepts.author_definition: The role prescribed for teachers in developmentalist theory, where they facilitate a child's natural growth rather than acting as 'authoritarian conveyers' of social knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Teachers as instructors or subject-matter experts.
concepts.source_passage: Teachers are to be developmental assistants, not authoritarian conveyers of unnatural impositions.

concepts.author_definition: The scientific concept that the neocortex is a blank slate awaiting social and cultural instruction, contradicting the idea of innate developmental stages.
concepts.common_alternative: The biological developmental stage model (e.g., Piaget).
concepts.source_passage: The neocortex, where schooling mainly takes place, is neither instinctual nor pre-programed... basically a blank page ready to be written upon.

concepts.author_definition: The developmentalist idea that human growth and learning follow internal, universal laws in each individual, analogous to biological growth.
concepts.source_passage: But these teachers’ textbooks leave the theory of “unfolding” quietly intact. We know now that the child does not “beautifully unfold, and grow well, in conformity with the laws which act in each individual.”

concepts.author_definition: In this context, the specific cultural and linguistic knowledge (traditions, moral imperatives, language) required to be a contributing member of a particular nation.
concepts.common_alternative: Ancestry, race, or hereditary cultural background.
concepts.source_passage: Rather, they are instructing children in an ethnicity – how to become mature, contributing members of a particular nation

concepts.author_definition: A theological concept applied to education, referring to the belief that human nature is inherently good and will develop perfectly if left alone.
concepts.common_alternative: A 5th-century Christian heresy concerning original sin.
concepts.source_passage: theologically speaking, developmentalism is an updated version of the Pelagian heresy that human nature if allowed to develop naturally is good and can be relied on.

concepts.author_definition: Active teaching from the outside, involving explicit transmission of knowledge, which the author argues is the only way to achieve literacy.
concepts.common_alternative: Often negatively associated with passive, lecture-style teaching or 'drill and kill' pedagogy.
concepts.source_passage: Active instruction of the child from the outside is the only literacy blueprint available.

concepts.author_definition: Non-instinctual, culturally-transmitted activities and knowledge that allow humans to adapt to diverse environments and outwit competitors.
Grave handicaps in language, nutrition, adult modeling, and the opportunity to gain key knowledge that children bring from their home life.
A hallmark constructivist classroom feature where children explore topics independently; characterized by Hirsch as a suboptimal 'profligate waste of time'.
The pedagogical approach where children are expected to build their own understanding through independent effort rather than being explicitly taught.
A society’s customs, its laws and moral restraints that hold a society together and constrain natural impulse.
Piaget's metaphor for the child, describing an individual constantly trying to figure out how her theory coordinates with data by herself.
A naturalistic, developmental approach to early knowledge-gain through working on actual projects, now often rebranded as constructivism or discovery learning.
The educational belief that all human development follows a fixed, predictable, and universal sequence of internal unfolding.

cases.name: Mid-Twentieth Century American Elementary Education
cases.description: The period during which individualistic developmentalist doctrines completely superseded earlier American views that emphasized content and adult guidance.
key_passages: the individualistic doctrines of de�velopmentalism which completely took over our elementary education by mid-twentieth century.

cases.name: COVID-19 Masking in America
cases.description: A contemporary example where the conflict between 'I' (individual annoyance) and 'WE' (group life-saving) demonstrates the dominance of individualistic thinking over communal cohesion.
key_passages: A COVID mask, for example, is an annoyance to the ‘I’ but it is a life-saving protection to the ‘WE.’ Based on Putnam’s data, I infer that an earlier, ‘WE’-oriented America, would probably have been less anti-mask.
contestable_aspects: The interpretation of anti-mask sentiment as purely a result of individualistic versus communal orientation.

cases.name: South Bronx Core Knowledge Schools
cases.description: A high-demand educational alternative in a low-income area where parents aggressively seek spots in schools that use a knowledge-based curriculum (Core Knowledge) over standard public kindergartens. According to Hirsch, this demonstrates that parents prioritize academic results and specific methods over the 'natural development' theories prevalent in public schools.
key_passages: parents of some 25,000 children in the South Bronx try every year to place their child in one of the seven Bronx Core Knowledge (CK) schools where there are just 135 kindergar­ten places.
Through their friends and their own observations, they have come to think that the kindergar­tens of the public school are a waste of valuable time

cases.name: COVID mask-wearing in America
cases.description: Hirsch uses the example of mask-wearing during the COVID-19 pandemic to illustrate the tension between individualism ('I') and communal responsibility ('WE'). He uses it as a contemporary illustration of Putnam's data on declining social cohesion.
key_passages: A COVID mask, for example, is an annoyance to the “I” but it is a life-saving protection to the “WE.” Based on Putnam’s data, I infer that an earlier, “WE”-oriented America, would probably have been less anti-mask.

cases.name: Mid-20th Century Classroom Transformation
cases.description: The author describes a fundamental shift in the physical and philosophical structure of American elementary classrooms, moving from fixed wooden desks arranged for teacher-led instruction to group tables designed for child-centered 'wandering' pedagogy.
key_passages: In grade schools across the nation, we have changed the composition and arrangement of the classroom furniture.
That's the look of the classroom under 'child-centered' education.

cases.name: A Nation at Risk (1983)
cases.description: A landmark 1983 federal report on the state of American education. Hirsch claims it was well-intentioned but flawed because its leadership (college presidents and corporate chairs) focused on high schools and ignored the foundational role of early elementary education and background knowledge.
key_passages: In one passage of the report there was a suggestion that elementary school teachers should be more knowledgeable, but on the whole, the elementary years were ignored

The study group for A Nation at Risk was controlled by estimable people who were presidents of top universities, chairmen of corporations, and high school principals. The college presidents set the tone. The analysis was high-school-centric.

cases.name: Massachusetts Common School System
cases.description: Under the leadership of Horace Mann in the mid-19th century, the system was the leader of the American school system. It was characterized by integration of free black students and a rejection of Rousseau's developmentalism in favor of socialized instruction to overcome human instinctual deficiencies.
key_passages: In Massachusetts, where leadership of the American school system resided, there was not even school segregation of black students.
The influence of the Massachusetts Common School spread out beyond the state by virtue of its brilliant leader, Horace Mann who, in 1837, was named Commissioner of Education for the state.
contestable_aspects: The degree of actual integration in practice
The characterization of Mann as purely anti-developmentalist

cases.name: Concord Nature Worship Movement
cases.description: The town of Concord, Massachusetts, home to Emerson and Thoreau, served as the epicenter for the 19th-century nature worship movement in America, which eventually influenced education via Elizabeth Peabody.
key_passages: The successors to Horace Mann in Massachusetts – especially in the town of Concord, – the home of Emerson and Thoreau – made the state also a cradle of the new nature worship in America.

cases.name: Concord, Massachusetts
cases.description: The home of Emerson and Thoreau, characterized as the 'cradle of the new nature worship' where developmentalist ideas were nurtured after the era of Horace Mann.
key_passages: The successors to Horace Mann in Massachusetts – especially in the town of Concord, – the home of Emerson and Thoreau – made the state also a cradle of the new nature worship in America.

cases.name: The dominance of developmentalism in US elementary schools
cases.description: The gradual adoption of developmental methods throughout the 20th century, which Hirsch links directly to the decline in reading scores reported in 'A Nation at Risk' and the persistence of low scores today.
key_passages: With the gradual triumph of ‘developmental’ methods in elementary school, all American reading scores – including those of white students – declined in the twentieth century.
Today’s reading scores remain low because of the dominance of developmentalism.
contestable_aspects: The causal link between developmental methods and specific score declines is debated by education historians.

cases.name: Disadvantaged 6th Graders in Effective Schools
cases.description: A comparative case where children from disadvantaged backgrounds who attended 'good, non-developmental' schools from kindergarten reached grade 6 having surpassed advantaged peers who were educated in 'child-centered developmental' programs.
key_passages: In fact, by grade six, disadvantaged children are ahead of their advantaged age group who have undergone a child-centered developmental education down the street.

cases.name: United States PISA Performance
cases.description: The United States performs poorly on international assessments, ranking 25th. The author attributes this specific failure to the nation's commitment to developmentalist educational theories.
key_passages: It’s the chief case of our poor showing (twenty-fifth among the nations on the international PISA rankings).

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: The principle that the child's 'instincts and powers' should guide her education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Dewey as the source of the principle that underpins developmentalism and argues it rests on a flawed, unstated religious faith.
key_passages: on the principle, stated by John Dewey, that the child’s ‘instincts and powers’ should guide her education.

thinkers.name: St. Augustine
thinkers.key_idea: The rejection of Pelagianism in favor of the view that humans are an 'infected mixture' of good and bad.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Augustine to illustrate the historical and theological roots of the 'mixed' view of human nature, aligning it with modern sociobiology.
key_passages: Augustine (a good observer of the dualism in humans) said: ‘Yes! We are still guilty of Adam’s disobedience and are still an infected mixture of good and bad.’

thinkers.name: Robert Putnam
thinkers.key_idea: Hyper-individualism has risen in America, weakening national cohesion.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Putnam's data from 'The Upswing' to support his claim that individualistic educational philosophy mirrors and contributes to a broader social decline in cohesion.
key_passages: Robert Putnam observes in his new book Upswing, which shows that hyper-individualism (the ‘I’ over the ‘WE’) has recently dominated in America

thinkers.name: Robert V. Kail and John C. Cavanaugh
thinkers.key_idea: Human development is generated by four forces: biological, psychological, sociocultural, and life-cycle forces.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their textbook as representative of the developmentalist indoctrination of teachers. He specifically critiques the inclusion of 'socio-cultural' forces as 'natural' developments.
key_passages: According to that view, human development is being generated by four forces: biologi­cal, psychological, sociocultural, and life-cycle forces. But note how un­natural is one of the listed forces – the “socio-cultural.”

thinkers.name: Harold Stevenson
thinkers.key_idea: Cross-cultural differences in basic mathematical achievement and number sense.
thinkers.engagement: Uses Stevenson's research to refute the idea that 'basic' development is universal and natural, showing it is culturally dependent.
key_passages: even basic development in something as neutral as number sense develops very differently in different societies, as Harold Stevenson and his associates have shown.

thinkers.name: Susan Carey
thinkers.key_idea: The origin of concepts as an arduous constructive mental process involving symbol manipulation.
thinkers.engagement: Cites Carey to provide a cognitive-science alternative to the 'organic' plant-growth metaphor of developmentalism.
key_passages: Susan Carey has shown that the 'growth' of concepts in the child is not an organic development, but an arduous mental process

thinkers.name: Lawrence Kohlberg
thinkers.key_idea: Universal eight stages of moral unfolding.
thinkers.engagement: Identifies Kohlberg as the 'chief moral idea' in developmental textbooks but dismisses the claim of universal naturalness in favor of tribal/cultural difference.
key_passages: The chief moral idea is that of Kohlberg’s universal, eight stages of moral unfolding... But the claim of universal naturalness is problematic

thinkers.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
thinkers.key_idea: A primary proponent of developmentalism in the early 20th century.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies him as a 'key player' in the adoption of developmentalism that led to current grade-school failures.
key_passages: Kilpatrick and Dewey were key players, but there was also another early player who may have been even more influential – Elizabeth Peabody

thinkers.name: Elizabeth Peabody
thinkers.key_idea: Introduced the developmental ideas of Friedrich Froebel to the United States.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch suggests she was perhaps even more influential than Dewey or Kilpatrick in establishing developmentalism in the early grades.
key_passages: another early player who may have been even more influential – Elizabeth Peabody, who introduced the developmental ideas of Friedrich Froebel.

thinkers.name: Horace Mann
thinkers.key_idea: Education must provide direction to human faculties because humans lack instincts for self-guidance and are born in 'darkness'.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Mann as the heroic counter-example to modern developmentalism, praising his rejection of Rousseau.
key_passages: Mann was the most imposing school official. He was advanced for his time.
The human being is less endowed with instincts for his guidance than the lower orders of animated creation.

thinkers.name: James Madison
thinkers.key_idea: Human nature is a mix of depravity requiring circumspection and qualities justifying confidence, a balance necessary for republican government.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch links Madison's view of human nature directly to modern sociobiology and evolutionary psychology to validate the founders' educational aims.
key_passages: Madison’s balanced view is essentially that of present-day evolutionary psychologists

thinkers.name: Benjamin Rush
thinkers.key_idea: The pupil does not belong to himself but is 'public property' and must be taught to prioritize the welfare of the country over family.
thinkers.source_work: Thoughts Upon the Mode of Education Proper in a Republic (1789)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Rush to show that the early purpose of American schooling was national allegiance and moral solicitude.
key_passages: Let our pupil be taught that he does not belong to himself, but that he is public property.

thinkers.name: Friedrich Froebel
thinkers.key_idea: Education should be like a 'children's garden' (kindergarten) where children grow naturally like plants.
